claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Remember, Child Benefit is assessed on a per-child basis.

First, could you tell me: **Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm responsible for my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me. I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! Let me work through Alex's eligibility with you.

Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), that's a good start. Now I need to ask a few more questions about his circumstances and yours.

**Is Alex currently working full-time (meaning 24 hours or more per week)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all. He's only 8, so he's just in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that makes sense. Now, the next question:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, thank you. Now I need to ask about your own circumstances. 

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. You mentioned Alex lives with you, which satisfies the responsibility requirement. 

Now, an important question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example, Alex's other parent or another family member?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to check if there are any special circumstances regarding where Alex is living. 

**Is Alex currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Living with foster carers?
- Or living normally with you at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's actually been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months or so. But he's still living with me normally otherwise, this is just a temporary hospital stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. When a child is in hospital, there are specific rules about Child Benefit. Let me ask you a few more questions about this.

First, regarding the hospital stay: **Has Alex been in hospital continuously for these 3 months, or has he had any breaks where he came home?** If he's had breaks, were any of them less than 28 days between hospital stays?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital continuously for these 3 months - no breaks where he's come home during that time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for 3 months (which is 12 weeks), this is an important consideration. 

Child Benefit can continue beyond the usual 12-week hospital limit if you're regularly spending money on Alex. This is an important question:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?** This could include things like buying him clothes, toys, treats, toiletries, or other items for his care and comfort.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy his clothes, magazines, and his favorite food while he's in there. I visit regularly and make sure he has what he needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, that's very helpful to know. Now, one final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs while he's in hospital?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards his hospital costs. It's just the NHS hospital care he's receiving.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why Alex qualifies:
- He is under 16 years old (8 years old)
- He's not working full-time
- He's not receiving benefits in his own right
- He's not in an apprenticeship
- You normally live in the UK
- You are responsible for Alex (he lives with you)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- Even though he's been in hospital for 3 months, you continue to regularly spend money on him (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means Child Benefit can continue beyond the usual 12-week hospital limit
- The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance

**Next steps to claim:**
1. Apply online or by post using form CH2
2. You'll need to provide proof of identity for yourself and Alex
3. You'll need Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate
4. Be aware that if you or your partner earn over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply
5. Payments are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

Do you have any questions about the application process or anything else related to Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Thank you so much! That's really helpful to know. I'm relieved that I can still claim even though Alex has been in hospital. I'll get started on the application straightaway. I appreciate you walking me through all of that!
